coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobes with antimicrobial properties and skin permeability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Demirci, Sahin
McNally, Alexandra B.
Ayyala, Ramesh S.
Lawson, Louise B.
Sahiner, Nurettin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) were prepared using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) as precursors via the hydrothermal method. Various weight ratios of PEI and CA, 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2, were used in the synthesis of N-doped C-dots. The N-doping of prepared C-dots was confirmed with an FT-IR peak that was observed at 1652 cm−1 assigned to N–H bending. The hydrodynamic diameters of prepared N-doped C-dots were 70.2 ± 6.9, 32.2 ± 4.7, and 11.5 ± 3.1 nm by DLS measurements, and surface charges values were 1.32 ± 0.8, 0.14 ± 0.2, and −1.57 ± 0.7 mV, for 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratio based on PEI of CA, respectively. A high quantum yield 53 ± 4.8% according to quinine sulfate standard was determined for 1:1 C-dot prepared from 1:1 wt ratio of PEI:CA. The C-dots were found to inhibit growth of both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria with no noted toxicity to mammalian cells in vitro. Skin permeation studies indicate that the particles permeate into and through the dermis within hours, indicating their potential use for topical administration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antimicrobial
Skin permeability
Biocompatible particles

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped C-dots from PEI-CA mixture
Synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic presentation of synthesis of N-doped C-dots from CA and PEI precursors.
The (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) TGA thermograms of N-doped C-dots synthesized at different ratios of PEI-CA precursors.
(a) The fluorescence-emission spectra of N-doped C-dots, and (b) their corresponding digital photograph images under sunlight and 366 nm UV light.

Inhibition of bacterial growth by N-doped C-dots. Bacterial growth of the indicted organisms was quantified in the presence (●) or absence (■) of N-doped C-dots after 24-h incubation. Data points represent mean ± SEM (n = 3).
In vitro permeation of N-doped C-dots through porcine dermal tissue. Permeation through the entire tissue thickness was quantified at indicated time points. Data points represent mean ± SEM (n = 3).
Zeta potential and DLS analysis of N-doped C-dots prepared from different weight ratios of PEI to CA.
The Q% values of prepared C-dot from PEI-CA precursors.
In vitro permeation parameters for N-doped C dots.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescent nanoprobes with antimicrobial properties and skin permeability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry & Nanoscience and Technology Research and Application Center, Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University Terzioglu Campus, Canakkale, 17100, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry & Nanoscience and Technology Research and Application Center
Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University Terzioglu Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, Tulane University School of Medicine, New Orleans, LA, 70112, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, Tulane University School of Medicine, New Orleans, Louisiana, 70112, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, Morsani College of Medicine, University of South Florida, Tampa, FL, 33612, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ophthalmology, Morsani College of Medicine, University of South Florida, Tampa, Florida, 33612, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Ophthalmology, Morsani College of Medicine, University of South Florida, 12901 Bruce B. Downs Blvd, MDC21, Tampa, FL, 33612, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) were prepared using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) as precursors via the hydrothermal method. Various weight ratios of PEI and CA, 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2, were used in the synthesis of N-doped C-dots. The N-doping of prepared C-dots was confirmed with an FT-IR peak that was observed at 1652 cm−1 assigned to N–H bending. The hydrodynamic diameters of prepared N-doped C-dots were 70.2 ± 6.9, 32.2 ± 4.7, and 11.5 ± 3.1 nm by DLS measurements, and surface charges values were 1.32 ± 0.8, 0.14 ± 0.2, and −1.57 ± 0.7 mV, for 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratio based on PEI of CA, respectively. A high quantum yield 53 ± 4.8% according to quinine sulfate standard was determined for 1:1 C-dot prepared from 1:1 wt ratio of PEI:CA. The C-dots were found to inhibit growth of both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria with no noted toxicity to mammalian cells in vitro. Skin permeation studies indicate that the particles permeate into and through the dermis within hours, indicating their potential use for topical administration.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, as one of the most abundant elements in the world, is widely available in nature, including in allotropes such as graphite, diamond and amorphous carbon. Recently, new generation carbon-based materials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon dots (C-dots), have been a subject of great interest due to their potential applications [26,36,45]. C-dots have almost spherical geometry [2] at nanometer sizes, consisting of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen atoms with intriguing optical properties that are sometime called as fluorescent C-dots. In recent years, C-dots have gained great attention due to their unique properties such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, water dispersibility and photoluminescence [11,12,15,39]. The combination of these unique properties in a single nano-point has made C-dots promising materials for versatile technological applications such as fluorescence marking, bio-imaging, drug delivery systems, sensors, medical diagnostics, bio-catalysis, bio-electronics, photo-catalysis and electro-catalysis [11,15,30,39,56,58,59].

The antimicrobial properties of C-dots have been investigated in several contexts and with various particle design and delivery strategies [58]. With the emergence of new antimicrobial-resistant bacterial strains, the use of biocompatible C-dots to target bacterial pathogens merits consideration. One potential use that has been considered is promoting the uptake of antimicrobial agents via conjugation to C-dot particles within the context of delivery to intracellular pathogens [1]. While the internalization of bacteria and pathogens inhibits the efficacy of typical antibiotics, intracellular delivery through C-dots has been found to promote intracellular uptake [1]. It was found that even in low concentrations, C-dots combined with the antibiotic metronidazole had increased antimicrobial activity when compared to metronidazole alone [1]. Photoactive C-dots have been found to cause oxidative stress in bacterium that can lead to the eradication of the organisms [33].

The direct antimicrobial activity of C-dots has also been demonstrated. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots have shown direct antimicrobial properties against Staphylococcus, in particular methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) [53]. When applied to wounds infected with MRSA, the results were comparable to vancomycin. Furthermore, the application of these particles was found to have negligible toxicity in vitro as well as in an in vivo rodent model. The carbon dots were shown to be able to cause the death of bacterial cells by causing rupture through interactions with negatively charged bacteria [53]. Pyrimidine-based compounds also lead to lysis of treated S. aureus and E. coli [35].

Silver nanolines and dots both had significant growth-inhibitory effects on S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms. A comparison of the different structures indicated that antimicrobial activity was associated with surface area rather than size or shape [13]. In using carbon quantum dot decorated ZnO nanoparticles in an injectable hydrogel, it was found to have antimicrobial properties [50]. The added benefit of the hydrogel is the zinc ions released from the hydrogel, which causes the antimicrobial effect to be sustained as well as furthers fibroblast growth. The nanoparticles decorated with carbon quantum dots have the capability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in 660–808 nm light, leading to antibacterial capability against S. aureus and E. coli [50]. Also, the various biomaterials for antimicrobial and antibacterial applications were summarized in literature [60] the selective antimicrobial effect of curcumin-loaded dextrin coated halloysite nanocontainers were also investigated [9], and the corresponding in vitro experiments showed that, while curcumin-loaded dextrin coated halloysite nanocontainers effectively inhibit the growth of S. marcescens cells, it had no effect against E. coli bacteria.

Many strategies such as laser-ablation [46], acidic oxidation [41], electrochemical synthesis [27], microwave synthesis [43], ultrasonic treatment [28], sono-chemical assisted synthesis [6], and hydrothermal synthesis [37] have been adopted in the synthesis of C-dots since their serendipitous discovery by the Xu group [51] in purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes. Among these methods, hydrothermal synthesis is particularly attractive and has been used intensively in the last decade due to its relatively mild reaction conditions compared to other techniques, as well as its inexpensive equipment requirements [47] and its superior properties [20,22].

Synthesis of C-dots with heteroatom doping allows tuning of optical properties and affords C-dots with high quantum yields [40,48,55]. Due to the size similarity between nitrogen and carbon atoms, it has been reported that nitrogen-doped C-dots exhibit better quantum yields [16]. In the synthesis of nitrogen-doped C-dots, amino acids [44], proteins [29], ethylenediamine [37] or other analogs [4,54] and polyethyleneimine [31] are prominent molecules that are widely used. The synthesized nitrogen-doped C-dots were used for in vitro [10,21] and in vivo [7,57] optical imaging as non-toxic and high-performance fluorescence imaging agents.

In the current study, the nitrogen-doped C-dots (N-doped C-dots) were synthesized using polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) precursor at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratios employing hydrothermal synthesis. The prepared N-Doped C-dots were characterized using Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), dynamic light scattering (DLS), zeta potential, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. To confirm the potential applicability of these particles in the prevention of infectious disease, antibacterial properties and mammalian cell toxicity were investigated. Considering the potential use of N-doped C-dots for topical application, we also established the skin permeation properties of these particles.

Citric acid monohydrate (Carlo Erba, >99%) as carbon source and polyethyleneimine (PEI, %50 in water, Mn:1800, Sigma Aldrich) as N- doping agent were used in the synthesis of N-doped C-dots. Quinine (suitable for fluorescence, anhydrous, >98, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a fluorescence standard. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 99%, Sigma Aldrich) was used in the preparation of the fluorescence standard for dissolving Quinine. Sterile phosphate-buffered saline (Gibco, Fisher Scientific) was used as received. Mueller Hinton broth (Criterion, Hardy Diagnostics) was prepared per manufacturer's instructions. Biosol was purchased from National Diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped C-dots from PEI-CA mixture

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of N-doped Carbon dots (C-dots) using polyethyleneimine-citric acid (PEI-CA) mixture was carried out through a hydrothermal method as reported in literature with some modifications [32]. In brief, the 2 mL of PEI solution (50% in water, Mn: 1800) was placed into a 50 mL beaker containing 25 mL water. Then, CA at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratios based on PEI were dissolved in 25 mL water. Finally, the prepared mixture was placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 250 °C for 4 h. After that, the obtained mixtures were transferred to a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut off ≥12,000 Da) to wash the prepared N-doped C-dots for 24 h by changing the wash water every 8 h. Then, liquid N-doped C-dots mixtures were concentrated via rotary evaporation.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo) spectra of prepared N-doped C-dots from various ratios of PEI and CA were recorded between 4000 and 650 cm−1 wavelength with 4 cm−1 resolution using ATR technique.

The thermal stability of the prepared N-doped C-dots from various ratios of PEI and CA were compared from thermograms obtained from Thermogravimetric-analysis (TGA, SII TG/DTA 6300, Exstar) measurements. The moisture from the samples was removed by heating them to 100 °C with 200 mL/min flow rate N2 gas. Then, the weight loss of N-doped C-dots as % weight loss versus temperature up to 750 °C was recorded with 10 °C/min heating rate in the presence of 200 mL/min flow rate of N2 gas.

The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of N-doped C-dots prepared from various ratios of PEI and CA was determined by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS, Brookhaven Ins. Corp., 90Plus/BIMAS) with a 35 mW solid state laser detector at an operating wavelength of 658 nm. Zeta potential measurements of the prepared N-doped C-dots were evaluated using a zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven Inst. Corp., BIC ZetaPlus) with DD water.
The N-doped C-dots prepared using various ratios of PEI and CA were visualized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7800, Hitachi) in a vacuum with an operating voltage of 100 kV.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns of the N-doped C-dots prepared from various ratios of PEI and CA were recorded by a PANalytical X'Pert Pro MPD diffractometer equipped with CuKα radiation and the X'Celerator detector on diffracted beam. The XRD data were collected in a Bragg Brentano (θ/θ) vertical geometry operating in flat reflection mode between 3° and 70° (2θ) in steps of 0.02° 2θ with 1 s step-counting time. The X-ray tube operating at 45 kV and 40 mA was used and 1/2° divergence slit, a 0.04 rad soller slit and a 10 mm fixed mask was placed in the incident beam pathway. The High Score Plus (v.4.6.0) software was used for peak identification and automated search-match to analyse diffraction patterns.
Optical properties of N-doped C-dots was investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy (T80+ UV/VIS spectrometer, PG Instrument Ltd), and Fluorescence Spectroscopy (Lumina, Thermo) at room temperature.

Quantum yields (Q) of N-doped C-dots were calculated by using Quinine sulfate solution as a standard. Quinine dissolved in 0.5 M H2SO4 was used as a standard with a known Q value of 54% at 345 nm excitation wavelength for quinine sulfate. Eq. (1) was used for the calculation of Q of N-doped C-dots.
(1) %Q = %QStd (I/IStd) (ODStd/OD) (ƞ 2/ ƞ 2 Std)
Where “Q” is fluorescence quantum yield, “I” is the integrated fluorescence intensity, “OD” is the UV–Vis absorbance, and “ƞ” is the refractive index of the solvent for C-Dots suspension solution as water (ƞ = 1.33) and 0.5 M H2SO4 in water (ƞ = 1.76).

Antimicrobial properties of N-doped C-dots were characterized in accord with the reported standard techniques [17]. Organisms tested included methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 4300), Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922), Staphylococcus epidermidis (ATCC 49134) and Bacillus cereus (ATCC 14579). To determine the minimum inhibitory concentration and growth curve characteristics, overnight broth cultures in Mueller-Hinton broth of each organism were diluted to a McFarland standard of 0.5. N-doped C-dot dispersions at appropriate concentrations were added to a final volume of 0.1 mL per well in a sterile 96-well culture plate. At specific time points during incubation at 37 °C, absorbance at 600 nm was read of both C-dot-treated wells and untreated control broth cultures of each organism with a microplate reader (Synergy HT, BioTek). Additionally, microplate wells were inspected for visual turbidity as compared to untreated controls for determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration after overnight incubation. From each well in which growth was inhibited, 10 μL was subsequently pipetted onto blood agar plates (TSA containing 5% sheep blood) to assess the bactericidal versus bacteriostatic properties of the particles against each organism.

Mammalian cell toxicity was quantified using human epithelial cell lines of breast and intestinal origin, specifically MCF-7 and T84 cells, respectively. An MTT assay was performed per manufacturer's instructions (EMD Millipore) by plating 15,000 cells per well in a 96-well plate and treating for 24 h.

Skin permeation experiments with N-doped C-dots were performed using techniques similar to our previous investigations [23,24,42]. Juvenile porcine dermal tissue (Stellen Medical, St. Paul, MN; shaved, nonperforated, split thickness at 1.5 mm) was stored at −20 °C. Upon use, the tissue was cut into sections of approximately 2 cm × 2 cm, thawed at room temperature, and mounted onto a jacketed Franz diffusion cell (surface area 0.64 cm2; PermeGear Inc., Hellertown, PA) with the stratum corneum facing the donor chamber. The receptor chamber, containing 5 mL of PBS, was maintained at 37 °C and gently stirred for the duration of the experiment. The donor chamber was filled with 200 μL of C-dots at 100 mg/mL for specified time points. A schematic depiction of a jacketed Franz diffusion cell is provided by Tsai and coworkers [49]. At the experimental end-point, aliquots from the receptor chamber were collected for fluorescence spectroscopy (Synergy HT, BioTek). For quantifying particle retention within the skin, individual tissue specimens were digested with Biosol for at least 3 h to release the retained particles. Fluorescence intensity of the solution was then compared to a standard curve of N-doped C-dots dispersed in Biosol. Permeation parameters, including flux, lag time, and permeability, were calculated as detailed in our previous work [24].

Data were analyzed using Prism 8 software (GraphPad Software Inc.,) by t-test for comparison of treated versus untreated cultures or linear regression for comparison of differences between growth-curve slopes. p-values <0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of N-doped C-dots from PEI and CA precursors were carried out by using the well-known hydrothermal method. In brief, 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratios based on PEI of CA were dissolved in 25 mL water and placed into an oven in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 250 °C for 4h. The schematic presentation of synthesis of N-doped C-dots is given in Fig. 1 . Although the mechanism of synthesis remains unclear, it has been suggested in the literature that N-doped C-dots are formed by the formation of a very large network in both precursor molecules with polymerization, since both CA and PEI contain multiple functional groups. Accordingly, N-doped C-dots are covalently bonded by amide (–NHCO–) linkages formed by thermal dehydration of ammonium carboxylate moieties through ionic interaction between carboxylic acid (-COOH) groups of CA and amine (-NH2) groups of PEI.

Thus, hydrophilic fluorescence N-CDs are formed with simultaneous progression of carbonization via incorporation of PEI into the reaction. It was reported that various functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxides, carboxylic acids, and amine on synthesized N-doped C-dots were observed [32]. The gravimetric yields for N-doped C-dots were calculated as 71.7 ± 3.9%, 82.5 ± 5.7%, and 89.1 ± 6.1% for 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratios based on PEI and CA.

The FT-IR spectra and TGA thermograms of N-doped C-dots were compared in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), respectively. In Fig. 2 (a), the FT-IR spectra of N-doped C-dots synthesized at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 ratios of PEI-CA precursors are almost the same showing very similar peaks for C–H vibrations at 2938 and 2862 cm−1, CO peaks at 1771 cm−1, N–H bending at 1652 cm−1, and C–N 1549 cm−1 stretching vibrations as expected indicating the formation of –CONR structure [52].

The TGA thermograms of N-Doped C-dots were also compared as illustrated in Fig. 2 (b). It was observed that N-doped C-dots prepared at 1:1, and 1:2 ratios of PEI to CA precursors were completely degraded at 570 and 595 °C, respectively. On the other hand, the N-doped C-dots prepared at 1:0.5 ratios of PEI to CA precursors was shown 95.4% weight loss at 640 °C implying better thermal stability amongst all the prepared N-doped C-dots.

The zeta potential and particle size of prepared N-doped C-dots were also measured and the corresponding results were summarized in Table 1 . The zeta potential values of N-doped C-dots prepared at 1:05, 1:1, and 1:2 ratios of PEI to CA were almost the same with 1.32 ± 0.8, 0.14 ± 0.2, and −1.57 ± 0.7 mV, respectively.

The sizes of N-doped C-dots were found to decrease with the increase in the amount of CA as 70.2 ± 6.9 nm for 1:0.5, 32.2 ± 4.7 nm size for 1:1, and 11.5 ± 3.1 nm for 1:2, respectively. The size of the prepared N-doped C-dots were also confirmed by TEM images, and corresponding TEM images of these C-dots prepared at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratios of PEI to CA precursors were given in Fig. S1 (a), (b), and (c), respectively. It is apparent that sizes of N-doped C-dots are in about 70–10 nm range and in accord with DLS measurements, with spherical shapes.
The crystallite sizes of N-doped C-dots synthesized at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 ratios of PEI-CA precursors were calculated as 1.0, 1.4, and 1.0 nm, respectively.

The fluorescent properties of N-doped C-dots were investigated by using Fluorescence spectrophotometry (Lumina, Thermo). The fluorescent emissions were recorded between 300 and 650 nm at 354 nm excitation wavelength with 500 V PMT voltages and the corresponding spectra were shown in Fig. 3 (a). As can be seen, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed for PEI:CA 1:1 C-dots that is at 439 nm with 42480 fluorescence intensity, whereas the fluorescence intensities of 31670 and 17730 were measured for 1:0.5 and 1:2 N-dopes C-dots at 440 and 445 nm emissions, respectively. Therefore, there is a red shift with the increase in the amount of CA in C-dots and a decrease in the fluorescence intensity.

From Fig. 3 (b), it was also seen from digital camera images that N-doped C-dots are transparent, whereas they were changed to blue color under 366 nm UV light irradiation. This confirms the fluorescent nature of this particles. It is apparent that the blue color intensity is less pronounced for 1:2 ratio, then 1:1, and 1:0.5 of C-dots prepared at weight ratio of PEI to CA.
The Q% values were summarized in Table 2 . The Q% values of N-doped C-dots prepared at PEI to CA ratios of 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 were measured as 31 ± 2.7%, 53 ± 4.8%, 7.6 ± 1.1%, respectively.

The increase in the amount of CA up to 1:1 wt ratio to PEI increased the Q% from 31 ± 2.7% to 53 ± 4.8% but decreased to 7.6 ± 1.1% after increasing the CA to 1:2 wt ratio of PEI. The reason for this can be attributed to the surface coverage of C-dots with excess –COOH groups coming from the excess amounts of CA as the negative zeta potential value, −1.57 ± 0.7 mV can also confirm this outcome.

The antimicrobial properties of the N-doped C-dots were assessed using medically-relevant Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. We first characterized the inhibition of early-stage growth of each organism in the presence or absence of N-doped C-dots at a constant particle concentration of 3.125 mg/mL in triplicate. At this concentration we noted no in vitro toxicity in either of two mammalian cell lines. The viability of both epithelial cell lines assessed was 100% following treatment with the N-doped C-dots at this concentration.

Treatment of each bacterial strain with the C-dots did result in differences in the rate of growth of each organism as compared to untreated cultures, as indicated in Fig. 4 . For each of the organisms assessed, the rate of change in bacterial proliferation (slope of the OD vs time curve) was statistically significantly different between treated and untreated cultures during the initial 5 h of incubation (p < 0.0001 for all organisms). All optical density readings were statistically different at the 5-h time point when comparing C-dot treatment to untreated cultures for each organism.

To quantify the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the N-doped C-dots, overnight cultures were incubated with serially-diluted particles in Mueller-Hinton broth in triplicate. The MIC for each organism, as determined by the lack of turbidity in the incubated culture, is indicated in Table 3 . There was little variation among the Gram-positive organisms. Methicillin-resistant S. aureus had a slightly higher MIC (0.3125 mg/mL) than the other Gram-positive bacteria, including methicillin-sensitive S. aureus (MIC = 0.188 mg/mL), S. epidermidis (0.195 mg/mL), and B. cereus (0.188 mg/mL). These values are similar in magnitude to those quantified for MSSA and MRSA treated with carbon nanomaterials assessed by Zhao and co-workers [53]. The Gram-negative E. coli was the most resistant to the N-doped C-dots, with an MIC of 1.56 mg/mL. Similarly, Zhao et al. [53] also noted lower sensitivity of Gram-negative bacteria and Enterococcus to their nitrogen-doped particles when compared to Staphylococci. Silver nanoparticles stabilized with a silicon nanocrystal shell exhibited a superior MIC value for E. coli at 14 μg/mL [18].

The permeation of N-doped C-dots through the entire dermal tissue was determined by measuring the fluorescence of the receptor chamber solution at 1, 3, 6, 18 and 24 h as shown in Fig. 5 . From these data points, the permeation properties of the particles were calculated as presented in Table 4 .

The lag time indicates that the concentration of particles in the receptor chamber reached a detectable level after 4.1 h, which is the minimum time required for particles to permeate completely across the dermal tissue. In comparing the calculated flux of N-doped C-dots to our previous skin permeation studies [24], the value was more comparable to the flux calculated for a lipophilic model dye, nile red, permeating through full-thickness porcine skin but much lower than for a hydrophilic model dye, sulforhodamine B. The permeability of the nanoparticles was over 200-fold lower than for the model dye molecules. After 24 h of treatment, 26.6% of the particles had permeated across the entire thickness of the dermal tissue (Fig. 5) and 15.5 ± 1.1% was retained within the tissue.

The retained amount within the skin is lower than amounts of lipid nanoparticles retained within porcine ear tissue [19]. While the permeation kinetics of carbon-based nano-dots has not been previously investigated, assessing our results in the context of similar permeation studies can inform future design of particles to optimize delivery retention into and/or permeation through the skin for specific applications. These particles show potential for treatment of local bacterial infections at the skin surface as well as within the skin. In addition, topical administration of N-doped C-dots could also be a means of achieving sustained release into systemic circulation because of their ability to penetrate intact skin [38].

Design of novel materials with skin permeability has attracted a great deal of attention in recent years for transdermal drug delivery as well as formulations for skin care products. Such materials are also useful for detection of occupational, environmental or consumer exposure to toxic materials [5,8,34]. Transdermal delivery systems can be tailored via chemical modification, making them electric field and ultrasound responsive [3] and rendering optical properties for diagnostic, sensing, therapeutic and actuating purposes. While conjugation of fluorescent probes has been suggested to track nanoparticles for targeted drug delivery [14], the inherent fluorescent properties of the prepared particles afford such advantages. Therefore, the prepared N-doped C-dots have the potential for transdermal administration by encapsulation or conjugation of specific molecules within or onto the particles. Furthermore, conjugation with specific protein, DNA, or RNA molecules would facilitate their use for diagnostic purposes. Each of these potential applications merits further investigation in future studies.

In this study, the N-doped C-dots were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal synthesis technique from PEI and CA precursors at various weight ratios. It was observed that the increase in CA ratio decreased the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential from 70.2 ± 6.9 nm with 1.32 ± 0.8 mV for 1:0.5 to 11.5 ± 3.1 nm with −1.57 ± 0.7 mV for 1:2 wt ratio of PEI to CA. The quantum yields of the prepared N-doped C-dots at 1:0.5, 1:1, and 1:2 wt ratio of PEI to CA were calculated as 31 ± 2.7, 53 ± 4.8, and 7.6 ± 1.1% according to the quinine sulfate standard. These particles inhibited bacterial replication, with a higher sensitivity noted by Gram positive organisms than Gram negative bacteria. The particles permeated into and through dermal tissue within hours of application. These properties establish the applicability of N-doped C-dots as novel antimicrobials. Their ability to permeate the dermis indicates their potential for topical delivery. In future studies, characterization of the mechanism(s) by which these particles interact with and inhibit bacterial replication will be a primary goal to inform future material design. Optimization of N-doped C-dots by expanding our understanding of their permeation route upon dermal delivery will be another focus of future studies.

Sahin Demirci: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Alexandra B. McNally: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Ramesh S. Ayyala: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Visualization. Louise B. Lawson: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Nurettin Sahiner: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this work from Tulane University School of Medicine is gratefully acknowledged. The authors would like to thank Claire Pierce and Katherine Hebert for assistance with experimental work. The authors also would like to thank Ms. Yildiz Yildirim for the great contribution for XRD studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jddst.2020.101889.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conjugated carbon quantum dots: potent nano-antibiotic for intracellular pathogens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ardekani, S.M., Dehghani, A., Ye, P., Nguyen, K.A., Gomes, V.G., 2019. Conjugated carbon quantum dots: Potent nano-antibiotic for intracellular pathogens. J Colloid Interface Sci 552, 378-387.
Baker, S.N., Baker, G.A., 2010. Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: Emergent Nanolights. Angew Chem Int Edit 49, 6726-6744.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Benson, H.A., 2005. Transdermal drug delivery: penetration enhancement techniques. Curr Drug Deliv 2, 23-33.
